commands.author: clawsweeper[bot]
commands.review_summary:
The PR moves service-mode stale gateway cleanup behind bind resolution and a `/healthz` probe, then adds a regression test for leaving a healthy existing listener in control.

Reproducibility: yes. from source inspection and PR proof: current main runs stale PID cleanup before supervised lock recovery, and the PR body includes redacted Raspberry Pi systemd logs showing duplicate service entries killing each other. I did not run the live Pi scenario locally.
commands.review_followup: The remaining useful improvement is a narrow test-only reinforcement of the systemd early-exit contract; the implementation itself appears aligned with current gateway locking behavior.

target.title: Fix gateway service startup race
target.body:
## Summary
- Fix a gateway service startup race where one service-mode gateway can kill another healthy gateway listener before the normal gateway lock/startup path runs.
- Move service-mode stale listener cleanup behind bind resolution and a `/healthz` probe so duplicate boot entries leave the already healthy gateway in control.
- Add a regression covering the service-mode case where a healthy existing gateway must not call stale PID cleanup or start a second server.

## Real behavior proof

### behavior
On the affected Raspberry Pi, two boot-managed OpenClaw gateway entries targeted the same port (`18789`). During startup, each service-mode gateway process treated the other gateway process as stale, killed it before the lock/startup path could arbitrate ownership, and then restarted. This produced a repeated boot/restart loop and made the gateway appear stopped or unstable.

### environment
- Host: Raspberry Pi running Linux/systemd
- Gateway port: `18789`
- Old duplicate system unit: `/etc/systemd/system/openclaw-gateway.service`, enabled before cleanup, OpenClaw service v2026.5.5, command shape `gateway --port 18789`
- Intended user unit: `~/.config/systemd/user/openclaw-gateway.service`, enabled through root linger, OpenClaw service v2026.5.12, command shape `gateway run --port 18789`
- Root linger: `loginctl show-user root -p Linger` returned `Linger=yes`

### steps
1. Checked live gateway processes with `pgrep -af '[o]penclaw.*gateway|[n]ode .*openclaw.*gateway'`.
2. Checked the gateway listener with `ss -ltnp 'sport = :18789'`.
3. Checked the system service with `systemctl status openclaw-gateway.service --no-pager` and `systemctl is-enabled openclaw-gateway.service`.
4. Checked the user service with `XDG_RUNTIME_DIR=/run/user/0 systemctl --user status openclaw-gateway.service --no-pager`.
5. Read the gateway file log at `/tmp/openclaw/openclaw-2026-05-18.log` for the repeated startup/kill sequence.
6. Disabled the duplicate old system unit with `systemctl disable --now openclaw-gateway.service`.
7. Rechecked process count, listener state, user service state, and `openclaw gateway status --deep`.

### evidence
Before cleanup, live process checks showed two gateway processes at the same time, one with command shape `gateway run --port 18789` and one with command shape `gateway --port 18789`.

The live gateway log showed repeated service-mode stale cleanup and termination. Representative redacted lines:

```text
killing 1 stale gateway process(es) before restart: <pid>
service-mode: cleared 1 stale gateway pid(s) before bind on port 18789
signal SIGTERM received
```

The old system service was confirmed as enabled and active before cleanup:

```text
Loaded: loaded (/etc/systemd/system/openclaw-gateway.service; enabled; preset: enabled)
Active: active (running)
CGroup: /system.slice/openclaw-gateway.service
  node ... openclaw/dist/index.js gateway --port 18789
```

After cleanup, the machine had one user-service gateway process and one listener:

```text
systemctl is-enabled openclaw-gateway.service
# disabled

XDG_RUNTIME_DIR=/run/user/0 systemctl --user is-enabled openclaw-gateway.service
# enabled

XDG_RUNTIME_DIR=/run/user/0 systemctl --user is-active openclaw-gateway.service
# active

pgrep -af '[o]penclaw.*gateway|[n]ode .*openclaw.*gateway'
# one process: node ... openclaw/dist/index.js gateway run --port 18789

ss -ltnp 'sport = :18789'
# LISTEN 0 511 0.0.0.0:18789 ... users:(("node",pid=<pid>,fd=25))
```

`openclaw gateway status --deep` then reached the gateway successfully:

```text
Connectivity probe: ok
Capability: admin-capable
Gateway version: 2026.5.12
Listening: *:18789
```

### observedResult
The operational issue was caused by a duplicate local service setup, but the code path allowed the duplicate boot entry to become a restart storm by killing a healthy peer before checking health/ownership. With this patch, a service-mode gateway first probes `/healthz`; if a healthy gateway already owns the port, the new process exits and leaves the existing gateway in control instead of running stale PID cleanup.

The regression test proves this behavior: when service-mode startup sees a healthy existing gateway, it does not call `cleanStaleGatewayProcessesSync` and does not start another gateway server.

### notTested
- I did not run broad local gates (`pnpm check`) on the Raspberry Pi because that is too heavy for this host and previously destabilized it.
- I did not reboot the Pi after the code patch because the duplicate old system unit was already disabled during operational recovery. The unit test covers the exact startup race this PR changes.

## Verification
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test src/cli/gateway-cli/run.option-collisions.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --write --threads=1 src/cli/gateway-cli/run.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run.option-collisions.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
